title: Diffusive Dynamics of Nonstabilizerness
abstract: Symmetries shape the quantum-information dynamics of many-body systems, but their effect on nonstabilizerness, the resource complementary to entanglement, is less understood. We compute the stabilizer Rényi entropy, a measure of nonstabilizerness, in $\mathrm{U}(1)$-symmetric one-dimensional random circuits. The disorder-averaged dynamics is captured by a four-replica tensor network, which we evaluate by $S_4$-adapted infinite time-evolving block decimation (iTEBD) directly in the thermodynamic limit. Together with a hydrodynamic argument, our results identify a diffusive universality class for the late-time approach of nonstabilizerness to its random-state value, with the stabilizer Rényi entropy gap closing as $1/t$. The same scaling is verified in an energy-conserving nonintegrable Ising chain. More broadly, our framework provides a hydrodynamic perspective on nonstabilizerness generation and offers insight into the design of approximate Haar-random states in Hamiltonian dynamics.
summary: This work analyzes the dynamics of nonstabilizerness, a key resource measure, in symmetry-constrained many-body quantum systems. Using advanced tensor network methods on random circuits and Ising chains, the authors prove that the approach to equilibrium follows a universal diffusive scaling law proportional to $t^{-1}$. This establishes hydrodynamics as a fundamental principle governing resource relaxation in such chaotic quantum dynamics.
main_problem: The study investigates how symmetries influence the dynamics of nonstabilizerness, a quantum resource complementary to entanglement, in many-body systems.
main_result: The late-time approach of nonstabilizerness to its random-state value exhibits diffusive scaling characterized by an algebraic decay $\Delta m_2(t) \propto t^{-1}$.
method: The analysis employs replica tensor networks and infinite time-evolving block decimation (iTEBD) techniques applied to the disorder-averaged dynamics in the thermodynamic limit.
model_or_system: The primary models are one-dimensional U(1)-symmetric random quantum circuits and energy-conserving nonintegrable Ising chains. The analysis is performed on the stabilizer Rényi entropy, which quantifies nonstabilizerness.
key_observables: Stabilizer Rényi entropy density ($m_2^A$), the gap to the Haar-random state ($\Delta m_2(t)$), and finite-size R\'enyi gaps ($\Delta M_q$).
important_parameters: The decay is observed in a diffusive window $	au \ll t \ll t_D$, where $t_D$ scales as $N^2/D$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the ensemble average restoring translation invariance, allowing for an iMPS representation in the thermodynamic limit.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the replica tensor network structure and plot the time evolution of the entropy density, confirming the late-time $t^{-1}$ scaling and higher-order gaps $\Delta M_q \propto t^{-q/2}$.
paper_structure: The paper first introduces nonstabilizerness via stabilizer Rényi entropy in U(1) random circuits. It then develops the tensor network machinery to calculate disorder-averaged dynamics, culminating in the identification of a universal diffusive scaling law confirmed by comparison with an Ising chain model.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete example of how hydrodynamic principles govern the relaxation of quantum resources (nonstabilizerness) in chaotic systems, linking concepts from entanglement theory to classical statistical mechanics universality classes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Disorder-averaged four-replica transfer network for |ρ⊗4 t ⟩⟩. Blue tensor: Haar-averaged U(1)-symmetric gate W [Eq. (5)]; yellow tensor: averaged initial state |ρ⊗4 0 ⟩⟩[Eq. (4)]. (b,c) Contractions with I⊗4 and Q defining two-site transfer matrices with dominant eigenvalues λmax;tr and λmax;Q, whose ratio determines the annealed entropy density. (d) Two-site update of the infinite matrix product state: apply W, restore canonical form, and truncate by singular value decomposition within the S4 blocks. (e) Annealed stabilizer R´enyi entropy density m2 A versus time. Inset: late-time behavior on a log-log scale.

FIG. 2. (a) U(1) random circuit: inverse gap ∆M −1 2 (t; N) for several N, linear in t in the diffusive window. Inset: post- Thouless exponential tail on a semi-log scale. (b) Higher R´enyi gaps in the same circuit at N = 24, plotted as [∆Mq]−2/q
